title: Police in B.C. say 652 victims in Canada's 'most prolific case of voyeurism ever prosecuted'
full_text:
Police in B.C. say 652 victims in Canada's 'most prolific case of voyeurism ever prosecuted'
Police say surreptitiously-recorded pictures, videos uploaded online and viewed around the world
A Vancouver Island man pleaded guilty in what police are calling the "most prolific case of voyeurism ever prosecuted in Canada."
The Saanich Police Department said investigators identified at least 652 women and girls as victims.
Saanich police say Yin Yeung Derek Chan, 39, was handed a 4.5-year prison sentence on Thursday after pleading guilty to multiple voyeurism-related charges.
Those charges include surreptitiously recording people in a place where they reasonably expect privacy, observing and recording for a sexual purpose, making child pornography and publishing a recording that was obtained in the commission of a crime.
Chan has also been added to the long-term sex offender registry.
"One thing I think that is important to recognize is just the effect that something of this level can have on a community, with 652 women and girls identified as being victims," Saanich Deputy Police Chief Damian Kowalewich told CBC News.
"We're alive to the fact that this has a greater effect than just even on those folks, it's just the community itself can get affected by something like this."
Police said they began investigating in January 2024 after discovering someone was uploading voyeuristic photos and videos to social media from somewhere in Saanich.
That led police to Chan, who had previously been convicted for voyeurism when he was caught filming a woman in a Victoria-area mall change room.
Police said they arrested Chan at his home in Saanich in April 2024, where they seized more than 28 hours of video and thousands of images captured between April 2017 and November 2023.
The material featured women and girls surreptitiously recorded at businesses, beaches, and in some cases through the windows of private residences throughout the Greater Victoria area, police said.
"We know that some of the material was shared online, including internationally. Our investigators have been taking steps to have that content removed," Kowalewich said.
"That process is very challenging. As many of us know, scrubbing the internet is virtually impossible. It's also one of the factors that increases the impact on victims."
Police also discovered that images of the victims were posted online, with many viewed on websites around the world. Formal requests were made to remove those images, police said.
Chan was released with conditions after his initial arrest, but was re-arrested in January 2025 and has remained in custody since.
Police identified and contacted 40 of the victims, some of whom provided victim impact statements.
Anyone who believes they may be one of the hundreds of unidentified victims in the case is asked to contact police at file950@saanichpolice.ca.
